div.h1: printk Log Levels: KERN_* -> Ring Buffer -> dmesg / Console Routing

div.p: Kernel ring buffer uses a spinlock logbuf_lock. Writing is non-blocking to prevent deadlocks during panic.
pr_debug("msg") -> printk(KERN_DEBUG "msg") (compiled out unless DEBUG defined)
Signature: int printk(const char *fmt, ...) Format: printk(KERN_INFO "hello: loaded, size=%d\n", sz) Expands via C string literal concatenation: printk("\001" "6" "hello: loaded, size=%d\n", sz)

ul.li: Remainder = developer format string No newline added automatically - must include \n

div.p: Type: Circular (ring) buffer - in-kernel memory Size: 512 KB - 4 MB (kernel config CONFIG_LOG_BUF_SHIFT) -> Default: CONFIG_LOG_BUF_SHIFT=17 -> 128 KB on most distros -> Check: dmesg --buffer-size Location: static array __log_buf[] in kernel .bss Records: Each entry stores:

ul.li: Facility (always 0 for kernel messages) Overflow: Oldest messages silently overwritten when full. Thread safety: Protected by logbuf_lock spinlock

div.p: 4 4 1 7 ^ ^ ^ ^ | | | |-- max_console_loglevel (never go below this) | | |------ minimum_console_loglevel | |---------- default_message_loglevel (for printk with no level) |-------------- console_loglevel <- THE GATE

Rule: Message is printed to console if: message_level (number) < console_loglevel Lower number = higher severity. Default console_loglevel = 4 -> KERN_ERR(3), KERN_CRIT(2), KERN_ALERT(1), KERN_EMERG(0) -> console -> KERN_WARNING(4), KERN_NOTICE(5), KERN_INFO(6), KERN_DEBUG(7) -> ring buffer only
sudo insmod mydevice.ko sudo rmmod mydevice
Condition: message_level < console_loglevel Default: levels 0-3 (EMERG, ALERT, CRIT, ERR) appear immediately on the terminal/serial console.
Condition: All messages, regardless of level. /dev/kmsg exposes the ring buffer as a structured byte stream. Used by systemd-journald -> journalctl -k.
Condition: All messages currently in buffer. dmesg invokes syslog(SYSLOG_ACTION_READ_ALL, ...) or reads /dev/kmsg.
